title: podman volume directive seems not to allow the container to write data
body:
/kind bug
**Description**
I am running an actual  Fefora F35 distribution (with enabled selinux). I want to run a little Weblogic testcluster for to solve some open config issues. My first milestone would be to run the admin node (Weblogic with the admin console), but the container seems to have problems to write into the given volume.

So the setup Dockerfiles haven been taken from https://github.com/oracle, The very dynamic domain folder has been linked to the local workDir directory. In order to allow the container (with its different user inside) i 
did the following:

```
$ mkdir workDir
$ chcon -t container_file_t -R $PWD/workDir
$ sudo setsebool -P container_manage_cgroup true
```

The i run the i was trying to run the node:
```
$ podman run -d -ti --name wlsadmin --hostname=node00 -p 7001:7001 --env-file $PWD/properties/domain.properties --env ADMIN_PASSWORD=weblogic00 --volume $PWD/workDir:/u01/oracle/user_projects:z --volume $PWD/properties:/u01/oracle/properties:z   12213-simple
```
As you may noticed i added a z to the volume directive. So what goes wong?
The container starts using the worDir volumen, because i notived, that a _domains_ appears inside the workDir directory, great ...  but i would expect now, that the defined domain would appear as a domain inside the workDir directory. 
In order to get some information i used podman inspect to find out, what has been configured:

```
$ podman inspect  wlsadmin  | less
....
...
       "Mounts": [
            {
                "Type": "volume",
                "Name": "c139c4fcf736f6985726135e06276be183238f0b7842a5b1c8fe0115e54b950a",
                "Source": "/home/cgroove/.local/share/containers/storage/volumes/c139c4fcf736f6985726135e06276be183238f0b7842a5b1c8fe0115e54b950a/_data",
                "Destination": "/u01/oracle/user_projects/domains",
                "Driver": "local",
                "Mode": "",
                "Options": [
                    "nodev",
                    "exec",
                    "nosuid",
                    "rbind"
                ],
                "RW": true,
                "Propagation": "rprivate"
            },
            {
                "Type": "bind",
                "Source": "/home/cgroove/Projects/asFreelancer/44_bayJustiz_virtual7/Dokumente/10_Projekte/LfSt_Bayern/20_Aufgaben/20220105_Queuing_Auslagern/20_Kodierung/10_DemoDocker/00_demoBase/001_simpleWlsNode/12213-domain/workDir",
                "Destination": "/u01/oracle/user_projects",
                "Driver": "",
                "Mode": "",
                "Options": [
                    "rbind"
                ],
                "RW": true,
                "Propagation": "rprivate"
            },
            {
                "Type": "bind",
                "Source": "/home/cgroove/Projects/asFreelancer/44_bayJustiz_virtual7/Dokumente/10_Projekte/LfSt_Bayern/20_Aufgaben/20220105_Queuing_Auslagern/20_Kodierung/10_DemoDocker/00_demoBase/001_simpleWlsNode/12213-domain/properties",
                "Destination": "/u01/oracle/properties",
                "Driver": "",
                "Mode": "",
                "Options": [
                    "rbind"
                ],
                "RW": true,
                "Propagation": "rprivate"
            }
```
So that is strange, why are there two bind structures, another one that links the desired destination "/u01/oracle/user_projects/domains" into another source? 
And yes, the domain appears under `/home/cgroove/.local/share/containers/storage/volumes/c139c4fcf736f6985726135e06276be183238f0b7842a5b1c8fe0115e54b950a/_data`

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. create a local directory and mark it  as container-usable with `chcon -t container_file_t -R $PWD/workDir`

2. run the container with podman run ... with the above prepared volume source

3. 

**Describe the results you received:**
I would exprect to see the domain-directory under my declared volume

**Describe the results you expected:**
The system mounted the container created domain directory under the host directory ``/home/cgroove/.local/share/containers/st`

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**


**Output of `podman version`:**
```
podman version 3.4.4
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**

```
[cgroove@dakar 12213-domain]$ podman info --debug
```

**Package info (e.g. output of `rpm -q podman` or `apt list podman`):**

```
#podman-3.4.4-1.fc35.x86_64
```

**Have you tested with the latest version of Podman and have you checked the Podman Troubleshooting Guide? (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md)**


No i only reply on the packages i received from Fedora

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**

comments.body:
This looks like Podman is getting confused and thinking the source volume is not a path, and creating a named volume.

Is $PWD an actual Path?

BTW No need to do the chcon stuff.  The :z will take care of that.

Could you try the mounts with an image like Fedora and see if it works correctly.

One potential issue is if the three mountpoints of the image are builtinvolumes, then there could be a bug in Podman where we don't notice that the bind mount volumes should take precedence over building volumes of an image.

I think the first volume is an image volume - can you provide a `podman image inspect` of the image you're running to be sure?

No two volumes are mounted at the same spot in the container, this is perfectly fine. I don't see any bug there? If both volumes were pointed at `/u01/oracle/user_projects/domains` or `/u01/oracle/user_projects/` we'd detect a conflict between the image volume and the user-requested bind mount and the user-requested mount would win, leading to only one mount; instead, since they target different directories, we layer them, and the more-specific mount (in this case the image volume) wins.

The best resolution is to change your bind mount to `/u01/oracle/user_projects/domains` instead of `/u01/oracle/user_projects`. That will cause it to be mounted instead of the image volume created automatically by the container image.
Great job. @mheon I am closing this since you have a solution.  We can continue discussion in the issues.  Will reopen if we actually find a bug in Podman

Thank you from my side for your quick reply and the helpful idea.
The domains directory was created by the container, so it was a good idea to do this from my sde and bind with the container path /u01/oracle/user_projects/domains.

Thanks!
